scenes.segment: Let's weigh up manual versus automated data pipelines. When it comes to speed, the manual approach is slow and hands-on, whereas automation runs instantly. And on reliability, manual work is error-prone, while automation validates every record. So the manual way gives you control but costs effort, and the automated way trades control for enormous scale.
scenes.rationale: A two-sided comparison (manual vs automated) where each side accumulates several short points across parallel dimensions (speed, reliability, control/effort vs scale). SplitscreenPointsV1 fits a two-column comparison with 1-4 short caption pills per side, which matches the multi-point pros/cons contrast better than the strictly single-caption ComparativePoints2 or the 1-2 sub-point YinYang2Points.
scenes.fitNote: Strong fit. The segment is a comparison of exactly two parallel things (manual vs automated data pipelines), answering the same questions from opposite positions. Each side reduces to a short list of 3 points (speed, reliability, and a control/scale tradeoff), well within the 1-4 pill limit and reducible to short captions. This matches "Use it when" (two parallel sides, each a short list). The one caveat is narration style, not fit: the source is interleaved point-by-point across sides (the BAD pattern in the guidance), so it requires the standard re-sequencing to side-complete order, which the Reprocessing directive explicitly calls the most common edit.
scenes.reorderNote: The source narration ping-pongs across sides dimension-by-dimension (speed: manual then automated; reliability: manual then automated; then a paired control/scale summary). The template reveals side A fully (title + all pills top-to-bottom) before side B exists on screen, so the interleaved order cannot be shown. I re-sequenced to side-complete linear: deliver the whole Manual side first (slow/hands-on, error-prone, control-but-costly), then the whole Automated side (instant, validates every record, scale over control). Meaning and tone preserved; no facts added.
scenes.linearizedVO: Let's weigh up manual versus automated data pipelines. First, the manual approach. It's slow and hands-on. It's also error-prone. And while it gives you control, it costs real effort. Now the automated approach. It runs instantly. It validates every record. And it trades that control for enormous scale.
warnings: Source narration was interleaved (point-by-point across sides); re-sequenced to side-complete linear per Rule 1. No parallel cadence used.

Icon ids are best-guess from the shared Small-Icons set; two carry the literal on-disk filename suffix '(1)' (user (1), search (1)) which may need normalising to canonical ids (user, search) if the loader strips the suffix.
All captions verified <=22 chars and titles <=40 chars. 'Control, but costly' = 19 chars; longest caption is 'Slow & hands-on' = 15 chars.

3 pills per side chosen; the summary clause was mapped to a third pill on each side rather than dropped, to preserve the control-vs-scale point. If a tighter 2-pill build is preferred, leftPill2/rightPill2 can be omitted.

scenes.segment: So how does an automated pipeline actually work? It has four stages. Of course it all ends with monitoring the live system once it's running. But first you ingest the raw data from the source, then you clean and validate it, and after that you load it into the warehouse.
scenes.rationale: An explicitly sequential four-stage workflow/pipeline (ingest, clean/validate, load, monitor) where order matters and each stage is a short label. Process5Steps is the workflow/pipeline template for 2-5 ordered labelled steps, matching exactly four stages.
scenes.fitNote: Strong fit. The segment is a sequence of ordered steps in a data pipeline (ingest, clean/validate, load, monitor), order matters, and there are 4 steps, within the 2-5 range. Each step reduces to a short label plus a concrete icon, and the flow runs straight through without branching or looping. This matches Process5Steps' "Use it when" exactly and triggers none of the "Do NOT use it when" exclusions.
scenes.reorderNote: The source narration is non-linear: it names the final stage (monitoring the live system) before any of the earlier stages, then backtracks to "But first you ingest... then clean and validate... and after that load it." Rule 1 requires strict execution order with no jumping to the end first. I re-sequenced so monitoring is spoken last, matching the canonical left-to-right build (step0 ingest, step1 clean/validate, step2 load, step3 monitor). Meaning and the four facts are preserved; nothing invented.
scenes.linearizedVO: So how does an automated pipeline actually work? It has four stages. First, you ingest the raw data from the source. Then you clean and validate it. After that, you load it into the warehouse. And finally, once it's running, you monitor the live system.
warnings: Labels were tightened to single imperative verbs (all <=14 chars, parallel form): 'Clean and validate it' -> 'Validate' to stay within the label rule; the full clean+validate nuance lives in the voiceover, not the chevron.
Icon ids (download, check, database, activity) are best-guess concepts; confirm exact ids exist in the shared Small-Icons set before render.

scenes.segment: One term you'll hear a lot here is idempotency. It simply means an operation can run many times over and still produce exactly the same result, with no extra side effects.
scenes.rationale: Names a single term (idempotency) and gives its definition. WordDefinition is the glossary/jargon-buster card carrying exactly one word plus its description, which is precisely this segment's shape.
scenes.fitNote: Strong fit. The segment names and defines exactly one term (idempotency) and follows the natural definition shape: say the word, then explain its meaning. There is no comparison, list, or process, and the definition fits within 2-3 lines. This matches WordDefinition's "Use it when" criteria precisely.
scenes.reorderNote: Already linear. The source already states the term first ("One term you'll hear a lot here is idempotency") and then delivers the definition, matching the canonical name-then-define reveal order. Only a light trim of the lead-in framing was needed for the slot text, not a reorder.
scenes.linearizedVO: One term you'll hear a lot here is idempotency. It simply means an operation can run many times over and still produce exactly the same result, with no extra side effects.

slots.text: An operation can run many times and still produce exactly the same result, with no extra side effects.

warnings: The setup step carries no narration; schedule it before the title so the chrome is dressed by the time the word types in.
Title 'Idempotency' is 11 chars (within 40). Description is ~104 chars (within the ~120 target for 2-3 lines).

scenes.segment: To recap the benefits of going automated: you get faster delivery, you make fewer errors, you can scale far more easily, and you end up with a clear audit trail of everything that ran.
scenes.rationale: An explicit closing recap ("To recap...") of four short parallel takeaways read top-to-bottom with no comparison or causal sequence. LessonSummary fits a closing recap of 1-5 short lines; CourseSummary is the alternative, but the lesson-level recap framing favours LessonSummary.
scenes.fitNote: Strong fit. The segment is a closing recap of a lesson's key takeaways, a flat list of four short benefit points, each reducible to a single short line. It is not an ordered process (the benefits do not have to happen in a fixed sequence) and not a two-way contrast, and it does not need a custom headline. Four recaps fall within the 1 to 5 range, so LessonSummary applies cleanly.
scenes.reorderNote: Already linear. The voiceover frames the recap up front ("To recap the benefits of going automated") and then lists the four benefits in straight top-to-bottom order: faster delivery, fewer errors, easier scaling, clear audit trail. No item is pre-announced and nothing jumps out of stack order, so wording is preserved.
scenes.linearizedVO: To recap the benefits of going automated: you get faster delivery, you make fewer errors, you can scale far more easily, and you end up with a clear audit trail of everything that ran.
warnings: The headline is locked to "Lesson Summary" and is not authorable; the title slot is shown for completeness only and is not a prop.

Captions were phrased as parallel noun phrases (all ≤32 chars). The source verb-led clauses ("you get faster delivery") were compressed to noun phrases to keep the pill stack parallel; the fuller phrasing remains in the voiceover.
